observations.recommendation:
We recommended for the conduct of a General Revision of Real Property Assessment for the current year and every three years thereafter, pursuant to Section 21 of P.D. No. 1621 to come up with an effective and efficient real property tax administration.
We further recommended that the Municipal Assessor submit an updated Real Property records to the Municipal Treasurer for the latter to update and prepare the required RPTAR/Taxpayer’s Index Card who shall thereafter, at every beginning of the year, furnish the Municipal Accountant with the duly certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year, which shall be the basis for the setting up of the receivable accounts.

1.9 We recommended that management:
a. Strictly implement the guidelines outlined in COA Circular No. 2020-
006 dated January 31, 2020, and the Memorandum issued by
management dated July 1, 2024, regarding the Physical Inventory Plan
(PIP) for the One-Time Cleansing of PPE to reconcile the variances
between the balances per books and the RPCPPE.
b. Assess the value of PPE items found at the station in case no such
information could be found in both accounting and supply records.
c. Make the necessary adjustments for the recognition of PPE items found
at the station and disposition of non-existing/missing PPE items, if
warranted, to reflect the correct PPE account balances;
d. Maintain updated accounting and property records; and
e. Finalize and submit the inventory reports and other supporting
documents to the Audit Team.
1.10 We further recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct GSO to issue
demand letters to the accountable personnel responsible for non-existing or
missing properties and instruct the Accounting Unit to set up receivable
accounts for those personnel who were unable to produce the PPE items, if
appropriate.
1.11 Finally, the RPCPPE report should state the condition of the properties,
whether it is serviceable, non-serviceable, obsolete, or not used since
purchased.

2.11 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct the Department Heads
responsible for maintaining goods intended for distribution to submit on a
monthly basis the distribution sheets and requisition slips directly to the
Municipal General Services Office for recording purposes.
2.12 We also recommended that the Municipal General Services Officer
consolidate and summarize the submitted RIS and/or distribution sheets
monthly, and submit them to the Municipal Accounting Office for proper
recording.
2.13 Moreover, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure that all
inventory issuances are recorded in the period they are distributed, supported
by an SSMI and a duly approved RIS.

3.7 We commended the municipal officials for their commitment to good governance,
as evidenced by the timely and full implementation of priority projects funded
under the 20% Development Fund, in strict compliance with the provisions of
DBM-DOF-DILG Joint Memorandum Circular No. 1.
3.8 We recommended that the concerned municipal officials continue to adhere to the
guidelines set forth in the aforementioned Joint Memorandum Circular to
sustain the effective utilization of the Development Fund and ensure the optimal
realization of the socio-economic development objectives identified in the
Annual Investment Plan.

4.11 We commended Management for its committed efforts in substantially
completing the identified Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs) as
stipulated in the Memoranda of Agreement for projects funded by National
Government Agencies (NGAs); and
4.12 For the remaining uncompleted PPAs, we recommended that the Municipal
Mayor direct the concerned departments to expedite the implementation of
the outstanding NGA-funded projects to ensure the timely delivery of their
intended benefits.

5.9 We commended the Local School Board of the municipality for its diligent
compliance with the reportorial requirements on the allocation and utilization of the
Special Education Fund, as mandated under Section 6.0 of DEPED-DBM-DILG
Joint Circular No. 1, dated January 19, 2017. We encourage the Board to sustain
this high level of commitment and transparency in the years ahead.
5.10 The LSB were thankful for the commendation and committed to continue to comply
with the required reports to be submitted on a timely basis.

6.11 We recommended that the GAD Focal Person, through the Chairperson of the
Technical Working Group, applies the HGDG test on its PPAs and use the
results as basis in estimating the cost to be mainstreamed in the Annual GPB
of the Municipality and for the Accomplishment Reports for GAD.

7.9 We recommended that the MDRRM Officer and the MSWDO Officer
facilitate the submission of the distribution list for the welfare goods paid
through Check No. 2083181 dated October 28, 2024, totaling ₱327,320.00, and
7.10 Moving forward, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure all
submitted distribution lists are complete and detailed to support the validity of the
transactions.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare journal entries to provide for the corresponding depreciation expense of the transferred infrastructure projects.

observations.recommendation: We also recommended to ensure the accuracy of computations for unexpended balances in the LDRRM Funds to prevent the occurrence of erroneous entries.

We recommended that resource for future claims of honoraria the supporting documents of the claim should be attached to the vouchers. Furthermore, we recommended that payment of honoraria should be supported with the necessary supporting documents as prescribed in COA Circular No. 2012-001.
observations.managementAction: In view of the submission of the lacking supporting documents these will be validated and verified. This is without prejudice to the issuance of Notice(s) of Suspension and Disallowance, if warranted.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that management should increase the amount of the bond of the Disbursing Officer from ₱100,000.00 to enough to cover her maximum cash accountability to protect the interest of the government in case of theft or loss.

1.6. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a) Require the GSO and the Inventory Committee to conduct the one-time
cleansing of the PPE accounts for CY 2024 as prescribed in COA
Circular 2020-006;
b) Direct the GSO and the Accounting Office to coordinate and conduct
the reconciliation of the results of the physical inventory and
accounting records, identify and address the nature and causes of the
variances and hold responsible the designated supply
officers/custodians and keep up reconciled reports at all times; and
c) Require the Municipal Accountant to:
(1) review the PPE balances together with the corresponding
depreciation and prepare a JEV for the necessary adjustments;
(2) record accounting entries for the recognition of PPE found at
station, if any, derecognition of lost/non-existing/missing PPEs,
writing off/dropping from the books of accounts of the
remaining amounts lumped under the unreconciled subsidiary
ledgers of PPEs, when authorized, and present the necessary
disclosures in the Notes to the Financial Statements;
(3) prepare the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to drop from the
books all infrastructure projects which were duly transferred
and acknowledged received by the recipients and end-user and
ensure that these are properly recorded in the LGU books; and
(4) drop from the LGU books the carrying amount of all
unserviceable properties, as soon as the authority to derecognize
is granted by appropriate office to bring the reported balance of
the PPE account in the financial statements in order.

2.11 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare journal entries
to provide for the corresponding depreciation expense of the transferred
infrastructure projects.

3.9. We recommended that the Designated Municipal GSO as head of the
inventory committee, perform the following actions, to wit:
1. Conduct a complete physical count of all inventory items semi-
annually, prepare and submit the Report on Physical Count of
Inventories (RPCI) to the Municipal Accountant for reconciliation and
to the Audit Team on or before every July 31 and January 31 of each
year for the first and second semesters, respectively;
2. Cause the preparation of the Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) upon
distribution/issuance of the stocks to the end users and summarize all
RIS in the monthly Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued and
submit the same to the Municipal Accountant for recording purposes
as attachment to the JEV.

4.14 We also recommended to ensure the accuracy of computations for unexpended
balances in the LDRRM Funds to prevent the occurrence of erroneous entries.

5.8 We commended the municipality officials for ensuring that the projects
charged under the 20% Development Fund are in compliance with the
provision of Item No. 3.2.5 of the DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 1, and the timely
implementation of the PPAs, and recommend that it maintain and sustain the
said positive practice.

8.9 We recommended that for future claims of honoraria the supporting
documents together with the detailed computation of the claim should be
attached to the vouchers. In view of the submission of the lacking supporting
documents these will be validated and verified. This is without prejudice to
the issuance of Notice(s) of Suspension and Disallowance, if warranted.

8.10 Furthermore, we recommended that payment of honoraria should be
supported with the necessary supporting documents as prescribed in COA
Circular No. 2012-001.

9.17 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Require the Municipal Budget Officer to allocate in the Annual
Appropriation Ordinance funds for Peace and Order (POP), as part of
its POPS Plan, as required in Section 4.1.3 of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-
DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01 taking into consideration that the total
amount for Confidential Funds (CF) shall not exceed thirty percent
(30%) of the total annual amount allocated for the LGU's POP;
b. Assume the responsibility as Special Disbursing Officer of the Confidential
Funds of the Municipality, or designate a regular/permanent employee of
the municipality for that purpose;
c. Require the Municipal Accountant to submit all Disbursement
Vouchers of cash advance with complete supporting documents to the
COA-Audit Team Leader within seven (7) working days from release
of the check, as well as the liquidation thereof in accordance of Section
6.2 of COA-DBM-DILG-GOCC-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01;
d. Approve and transmit quarterly accomplishment report on the use of
Confidential Funds to the Secretary of the DILG, copy furnished COA;
and
e. Ensure compliance with COA-DBM-DILG-GOCC-DND Joint
Circular No. 2015-01 and related issuances of government oversight
bodies on Confidential Funds.

9.18 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Record the cash advance in the books of accounts and maintain a
separate subsidiary record of cash advances for Confidential Funds per
Special Disbursing Officer; and
b. Record the liquidation in the books within five days after the receipt of
the copy of the transmittal of documents duly acknowledged by the
Intelligence and Confidential Funds Audit Unit (ICFAU) and submit
the corresponding Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to the COA-ATL.

10.10 We recommended that management should increase the amount of the bond
of the Disbursing Officer from P100,000.00 to P 3,500,000.00 enough to cover
her maximum cash accountability to protect the interest of the government in
case of theft or loss.

1.10 We recommended for the conduct of a General Revision of Real Property
Assessment for the current year and every three years thereafter, pursuant to
Section 21 of P.D. No. 1621 to come up with an effective and efficient real
property tax administration.

1.11 We further recommended that the Municipal Assessor submit an updated Real
Property records to the Municipal Treasurer for the latter to update and
prepare the required RPTAR/Taxpayer’s Index Card who shall thereafter, at
every beginning of the year, furnish the Municipal Accountant with the duly
certified list showing the name of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible
for the year, which shall be the basis for the setting up of the receivable
accounts.

2.18 We recommended that the Designated Municipal GSO as head of the
inventory committee, perform the following actions, to wit:
1. Conduct a complete physical count of all inventory items at the end of
the year, prepare and submit the Report on Physical Count of
Inventories (RPCI) to the Municipal Accountant for reconciliation.
2. Cause the preparation of the Requisition and Issue Slip (RIS) upon
distribution/issuance of the stocks to the end users and summarize all
RIS in the monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued and submit
the same to the Municipal Accountant for recording purposes.

2.19 We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant stop the practice of
recording immediately to the expense account the inventories received by the
municipality. Recognize the corresponding expense account based on the monthly
Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued, supported by duly approved RIS.

2.20 The Municipal Accountant and GSO were amenable to the audit recommendations
and assured the strict compliance thereof.

3.10 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record all the transactions
of the municipality using the proper accounts provided in the Revised Chart
of Accounts for LGUs so that transactions are faithfully and correctly
presented in the financial statements to provide reliable information to its
users.

4.7 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare a Journal Entry
Voucher to restore the amount of unreleased checks to the Cash in Bank
account balance based on the Schedule of Unreleased Checks submitted by the
Municipal Treasurer to reflect the actual and correct Cash balance and
Accounts Payable of the Municipality at the end of every year.

6.12 We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all personnel concerned
continuously adhere to the provisions of DBM-DOF-DILG Joint
Memorandum Circular No. 1, dated November 4, 2020 to ensure that
maximum attainment of desired socio-economic development and
environmental management outcome of the municipality is achieved, and for
the realization of the intended purpose of the 20% Development Fund.

7.9 For the unexpended balance of ₱485,439.83, we recommended that the
Municipal Mayor require the Municipal Accountant to prepare the
Disbursement Vouchers for the return of the unexpended amount from
completed projects to the Source Agencies and submit the required
Liquidation Reports to the funding agencies concerned or request the transfer
thereof to the General Fund of the municipality, if the disposition of the
unexpended balances is not provided in the MOA.

8.14 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Chairman of the MDRRM
Council, through the MDRRM Officer:
1. ensure that all PPAs aligned with the Municipality’s LDRRMFIP
would be implemented and completed within the timeframe indicated
in the plan;
2. conduct close monitoring and evaluation on ongoing projects to identify
any factor that impedes the efficient implementation of PPAs and
introduce interventions, whenever necessary;
3. re-examine and/or re-evaluate the unimplemented PPAs to identify the
causes of non-implementation and address the same. Consider revising
the Plan, if necessary;
4. coordinate with the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) and other
Department concerned to fast-track the procurement process;
5. optimally utilize the LDRRM Fund to adequately equip the
constituents in their preparation to manage disaster risks towards a
resilient community in times of calamities.

9.13 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor strictly observe the provisions of
Section 62.1 of the 2016 RIRR of R.A. No. 9184, as amended by GPPB
Resolution No. 30-2017, dated May 30, 2017 in imposing warranty security in
order to protect not only the interest of the government but also those of the
third parties involved in the execution of the government projects, programs,
and activities. Otherwise, expenses or losses incurred due to the defective
goods within the warranty period be charged to the concerned officials
approving payment.

9.14 We further recommended that the Municipal Mayor should assign only
somebody who knows how to operate and handle the speedboat. He should be
trained in the proper use of the speedboat to avoid the possible damage to the
property.

10.16 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor prioritize the provision of an
adequate and proper permanent storage area for all welfare goods purchased
by the municipality to ensure that the items are safeguarded against any loss,
pilferage and wastage.

11.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, HRMO Head, and other
Department Heads concerned to:
1. Submit justification on the hiring of 130 JO personnel;
2. Limit the hiring of JO personnel to complement only the office where
transactions are voluminous; and
3. Submit a Contract of Service for each JO personnel and ensure that the
functions and responsibilities of the JOs are well-defined and clear in
their respective Contracts of Service to facilitate evaluation of work
performed by the employees.

11.7 The management is currently implementing the Audit Team’s recommendation by
issuing Executive Order No. 18 dated March 23, 2023, prescribing the guidelines
on the hiring of job order personnel based on CSC Resolution No. 021480, effective
April 1, 2023.

12.12 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor in coordination with the
Sangguniang Bayan, adopt and implement guidelines to rationalize and
regulate the provision and use of the cellular/load allowance taking into
consideration the following:
1. To submit legal authorization on the grant of load/cell card allowances.
2. Review the amount of cell card load allowance in relation to the
available landline and internet connection and the reduction of the
amount to a minimum level since these should only be used in the
performance of their official duties.
3. The official/employee who should be entitled to it and the amount to be
granted should be limited only for his official phone calls.

12.13 The management is amenable to the audit recommendations and committed to
adopt a guideline in the issuance of the reimbursable load/cellular allowance.

10. Conduct actual physical count on all inventories on hand;
11. Prepare RPCI and the Municipal Accountant for the preparation of journal entries to set the accounts in order.
12. Through the Local Finance Committee appropriate funds for the construction of storeroom/bodega to store its supplies and materials; and
13. General Services Officer should perform its function as custodian of all properties owned by the LGU and those granted in the form of donations and assistance.
14. Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant should adopt the Perpetual System of accounting for inventories and maintain the Supplies Ledger Card in the prescribed format to record acquisition, issuance and disposal. Likewise, the Supply Officer should maintain stock cards for each kind of inventory item to record receipts and issuances of stock.
observations.managementAction: Only physical count of office supplies was done for the year 2021. There are inventory items which were not counted at yearend hence not recorded. Consolidated RSMIs were not prepared. The MGS Officer admitted difficulty in maintaining stock cards, ledger cards and reconciling same because supplies issued directly to municipal offices. The LGU has been conducting annual physical count but results were no longer reflected in the books of accounts. With completion of LGU warehouse and computerization of LGU Inventory System the recommendations will be complied with.
observations.reason: Lacking storeroom/bodega is still the main problem of the LGU as of this report. The various offices were oriented on the process of the receipt, recording, issuance and reporting for in-kind donations. Perpetual system of accounting was not maintained. Consolidated RSMIs were not prepared. Supplies perpetual ledger cards maintained in the GSO had zero balances.

observations.recommendation: 27. Conduct physical count of all the remaining donated items and issue ICS on semi-expendable donations in-kind; 28. Prepare inventory list of the remaining donated items every month or every quarter if the agency is under quarantine for more than three months and determine the condition of these items; 29. Henceforth, prepare all the reports and documents required to account and monitor the receipt and issuances of all donations in kind and submit a copy of all the reports to the Audit Team.
observations.managementAction: Noted for forthcoming submission of documents. The Municipal Supply Officer was oriented on the process. The offices were oriented on receipt, recording, issuance and reporting for in-kind donations. The Inventory list from DRRM will be prepared and copies forwarded to COA.

observations.recommendation:
1.10. We recommended that the Municipal Inventory, Inspection, Appraisal and
Disposal Committee submit justification for the disposal of 15 motor vehicles
and heavy equipment without adhering to the required processes per COA
Circular 89-296.
1.11. We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant restore the balances
of each PPE item derecognized without valid supporting documents in
accordance with COA Circular 2020-006 to afford fair presentation of the
related PPE accounts in the financial statements; and
1.12. Finally, we recommended that the General Services Officer (GS Officer),
Inventory Committee, Municipal Accountant and other LGU Officials
concerned shall conduct cleansing of PPE balances following the guidelines
provided in COA Circular 2020-006 to bring the balances of each specific PPE
item in the books of accounts in order.

2.5. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare accounting entries
for transactions in the Special Trust Fund in accordance with the prescribed
standards in order to present reliable information in the financial statements.

3.16. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant record all lnventories
actually found on hand as of December 31, 2021, to afford fair presentation of
the related Inventory Accounts in the Financial Statements.
3.17. The GSO should segregate welfare goods inventories pertaining to
procurement made in CY 2021 from those procured in CY 2022 so as to
determine the balance of inventories to be accounted as of December 31, 2021
by the Accounting Office.
3.18. We also recommended that the MGS Officer:
3.16.1 maintain an updated and reconciled stock cards for each type of
inventory item, and ensure that issuances of supplies are duly
documented with RIS which shall be consolidated weekly for the
preparation of SSMI for all supplies and materials issued. The SSMI
together with the original copy of the RIS shall be submitted to the
Municipal Accountant every end of the month; and
3.16.2 conduct semestral and annual physical count of all inventory items and
prepare the Report of Physical Count of Inventories and submit to the
Accounting Office and to the Audit Team within the prescribed period.
3.19. We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
3.17.1 reconcile the supplies ledger cards with the stock cards of the MGS
Officer;
60
3.17.2 prepare JEV to recognize expenses for inventories utilized based on the
submitted SSMI supported with the RIS from the MGS Office; and
3.17.3 require Distribution Lists for Welfare Goods for Distribution as one of
the supporting documents in the recording of expenses.

4.11. We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare and submit to the
Municipal Accountant the Schedule of Unreleased Checks as basis to restore
the amounts referred to in paragraph 4.4 to the Cash in Bank account.
4.12. Also, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare as soon as
possible a Journal Entry Voucher to restore the amount of unreleased checks
to the Cash in Bank account balance based on the schedule of unreleased
checks submitted by the Municipal Treasurer to reflect the actual cash balance
and Accounts Payable of the Municipality at the end of the year.

5.6. We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
5.6.1 send written notice to the payees concerned at least one month before
the check become stale; and
5.6.2 periodically coordinate with the Municipal Accounting’s Office
regarding the existence of the stale checks.
5.7. Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant to take up the
necessary adjustment for stale checks in the books of accounts of the LGU.

6.14. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the personnel in-charge
of the operations of the respective LEEs to:
6.15.1 prepare the following documents to comply with the legal
provisions and other requirements governing LEEs, such as:
• 5-Year Staffing Plan and Staff Transition Strategy
• 5-Year Business Plan for each LEE
6.15.2 prepare and submit Physical Report of Operations semi-annually
to the Local Finance Committee;
6.15.3 conduct a semi-annual financial sufficiency assessment review
following the steps outlined in the LEE Manual and prepare the
SAFSSR Reports;
6.15.4 Use the SAFSSR Report in improving the operations of the LEEs to
become self-sufficient, to increase source of local income and
consider cost-cutting measures to prevent the incurrence of losses.

7.7. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor adhere to the provision
prescribed in GPPB Resolution No. 30-2017, dated 30 May 2017 and require
the Municipal Accountant to deduct retention money in an amount equivalent
to at least one percent (1%) but not to exceed five percent (5%) of every
progress payment of goods or require the Municipality’s suppliers/
contractors of goods to post a special bank guarantee equivalent to at least one
percent (1%) but not to exceed five percent (5%) of the total contract price, in
order to protect not only the interest of the government but also those of the
third parties involved in the execution of government projects, programs or
activities, otherwise, expenses due to the defects of goods within warranty
period be charged to the concerned officials approving payment.

8.10. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor should remind the awardees of
the project to identify a project and implement it as soon as possible to
optimize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund.
8.11. We also recommended that the Municipal Agriculturist to extend technical
assistance to the Punong Barangays concerned for the immediate
identification and implementation of the agricultural-related livelihood
project.

9.19. We recommended that the:
9.19.1 RHU personnel as well as the BHWs submit the lacking supporting
documents/ records/ orders to the Audit Team to support payments of
AHDP and SRA and accordingly observe compliance on the
documentary requirements for these kinds of personnel incentives to
avoid the issuance of a Notice of Suspension in audit; and
9.19.2 Municipal Accountant, henceforth, ensure that all necessary
supporting documents are attached to these claims before payments are
made.

10.13. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor through the Municipal Social
Welfare Development Officer, BAC Chairman submit a justification for the
Audit Team’s evaluation on the implementation of supplemental feeding using
CY 2018 funds from DSWD without adhering to DSWD Administrative Order
No. 04, series of 2010 or the Amended Omnibus Guidelines in the
Implementation of the Supplementary Feeding Program.
10.14. We also recommended that for similar transaction in the future, the Municipal
Mayor direct all local officials concerned to follow the Community
Participation Procurement Manual (CPPM) in the organization of small
farmers of the LGU to participate in the delivery of indigenous foods or locally
produced foods to be used as raw food ingredients in the implementation of
Supplemental Feeding Program of DSWD.

1. Review the accounting entries made on the 15% Mobilization Fee, reclassification of accounts, and prior period errors (color red) and make adjusting entries for identified errors.
2. Record the interest income temporarily under the account Due to Other Funds and cause the transfer at year end to the General Fund.
3. Record as income under the account Transfers from National Government the actual disbursement (utilization) upon liquidation instead of direct recording to Equity Account.
4. Consider the adjusting entries presented in the AAR.
observations.managementAction: Adjustment/corr made on the 15% Mobilization Fee, reclassification of accounts, and prior period errors (color red) and make adjusting entries for identified errors.
Adjustment/corr were made per JEV No. 2140 dated June 30, 2021 to adjust and correct the books.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to record subsequent procurement of Inventories, recognize the related items on hand. The Supply Officer prepare the consolidated RSMI every 15th day of the month based on the RSMI received from the end-user office and submit the consolidated RSMI to the Accounting Office for recording.
observations.managementAction: All procurement made were automatically issued to office due to lack of GSO bodega as alleged. Consolidated RSMIs were not prepared. The supply ledger cards maintained have zero balances. The LGU is completing the warehouse and computerizing the inventory system.

observations.recommendation: The Municipal Mayor (Chairman of the Development Council) should see to it that desirable projects to be funded in the annual 20% DF are well planned and procurement-implementation ready. The Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator should monitor and evaluate the implementation of the different projects and render a report whether all these projects were implemented in accordance with the approved development plan. In the preparation of AIP specify projects with particularity; secure authority from Sangguniang Bayan when project not included in appropriation ordinance. Municipal Engineer to submit Program of Work to BAC Secretariat for projects. Revert savings from completed projects to unappropriated surplus and reprogram pursuant to Section 322 RA 7160.
observations.managementAction: Starting the year 2021, the various PPAs of the LGU were closely monitored. Thereafter, 9 out of 11 PPAs listed under the 20% Development Fund have been implemented. One is still on-going and only one remain to be unimplemented as of September 2021. The AIP for CY 2021 contains projects which did not indicate specific identification of project for implementation. The Municipal Engineer was recommended to submit programs of work. The savings were already reverted and reappropriated to other development projects.

observations.recommendation: 22. Separate the LDRRMF Prior Years from the General Fund and recognize it under Trust Fund book; 23. Prepare and maintain the subsidiary ledgers for the special trust fund by year of transfer; 24. Prepare the Disbursement Voucher immediately to transfer the current unexpended LDRRMF amounting to ₱2,795,697.22 to the appropriate account under the Trust Fund; 25. Henceforth, prepare the accounting entries for LDRRMF transactions in accordance with the prescribed standards.
observations.managementAction: Correct the books. Proper books and JEV No. 08 dated July 27, 2021 to correct the books. Subsidiary Ledgers were prepared for the STF. The Disbursement Voucher for transfer was done. The LDRRMF will be presented in the Investment Plan. The LDRRMF transactions will be recorded per prescribed standards.

observations.recommendation: 26. We recommended The LDRRMO should present in lump sum the 30% Quick Response Fund in the LDRRM Investment Plan in accordance with existing guidelines and charge capital outlay for 30% Quick Response Fund in the 2021 MDRRM Fund allocation. Furthermore, the MDRRM council reprogrammed the fund allocation.
observations.managementAction: The 2022 Annual Budget for MDRRM Fund was updated reflecting all the expenditures. The PPAs of the LGU were closely monitored and the MDRRM Council reprogrammed the allocation. The 30% Quick Response Fund in the LDRRM Investment Plan was reflected accordingly.

observations.recommendation: 30. The Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction Management Officer should prepare the Cash Donation Utilization Request (CDUR) for charges to or utilization of the fund and attach the original copy to the Disbursement Voucher (DV) and furnish the Budget and Accounting Office with one copy each; 31. The Municipal Accountant should furnish the Municipal Budget Officer copies of the RCD, Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) and credit memo relating to cash donations; 32. The Municipal Budget Officer use the copies as bases in the preparation of the Registry of Cash Donations and Utilizations (RCDU). The RCDU shall be maintained per donor per purpose and updated regularly by the Budget Unit; 33. The Accounting Unit should prepare and maintain separate subsidiary ledger (SL) for "Trust Liabilities-DRRMF" for cash donations received amounting to ₱100,000.00 and above per donor per purpose while those below ₱100,000.00 shall be grouped and posted in one SL labeled as "Various Donors" per purpose.
observations.managementAction: Implemented. The Donated Cash Utilization forms were prepared where applicable. The Municipal DRRM Officer noted for forthcoming submission. The various offices were oriented. The Municipal Accountant will furnish copies of RCD, JEV and credit memo to Budget Officer.

observations.recommendation: 34. observations.recommendation:
We recommend that the Municipal Accountant should:
Review the accounting entries made on the 15% Mobilization Fee, reclassification of accounts, and prior period errors (color red) and make adjusting entries for identified errors.
Record the interest income temporarily under the account Due to Other Funds and cause the transfer at year end to the General Fund.
Record as income under the account Transfers from National Government the actual disbursement (utilization) upon liquidation instead of direct recording to Equity Account.
Consider some of the following adjusting entries:

We recommend that the Inventory Committee should:
Conduct actual physical count on all inventories on hand, and
Prepare RPCI and submit to the Municipal Accountant for the preparation of journal entries to set the accounts in order.
We also recommend that the Municipal Mayor:
through the Local Finance Committee appropriate funds for the construction of adequate storeroom/bodega to store its supplies and materials; and
General Services Officer should perform its function as custodian of all properties owned by the LGU and those granted in the form of donations and assistance.
Moving forward, we recommend that the Municipal Accountant should adopt the Perpetual System of accounting for inventories and maintain the Supplies Ledger Card in the prescribed format to record acquisition, issuance and disposal.  Likewise, the Supply Officer should maintain stock cards for each kind of inventory item to record receipts and issuances of stock.

We recommend that Municipal Mayor direct the Municipal Accountant to record subsequent procurement of supplies and materials in the proper Inventory account upon receipt of the delivery and recognize the related expenses based on the Report of Supplies and Materials Issued (RSMI) from the Supply Officer duly supported with pre-numbered Requisition and Issue Slips.
We also recommend that the Supply Officer prepare the consolidated RSMI every 15th day of the month based on the RSMI received from the end-user offices and submit the consolidated RSMI to the Accounting Office for recording.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, who is the chairman of the Municipal Development Council should see to it that proposed projects to be funded in the annual 20% DF are well-planned and procurement and implementation-ready.
We also recommended that the Municipal Planning and Development Coordinator should:
monitor and evaluate the implementation of the different projects and activities and render a report whether these projects were implemented in accordance with the approved development plan;
in the preparation of AIP, specify the projects to be implemented or goods to be procured in such details specifying with particularity, such that all that the local chief executive needs to do is to execute the contract. Otherwise, secure authority from the Sangguniang Bayan prior to entering into a contract when a project is not included or specifically provided in the Municipal’s appropriation ordinance to avoid legal repercussion.
We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer submit to the BAC Secretariat the Program of Work of all projects identified in the AIP especially those funded in prior years so that the same can be scheduled for bidding.
Lastly, we recommended that all savings derived from completed projects in prior years be reverted to the unappropriated surplus and re-programmed to other projects upon enactment by the Sangguniang Panlungsod, rather than retaining them as Continuing Appropriation, pursuant to Section 322 of RA 7160.

We recommended that the LDRRMO should present in lump sum the 30% Quick Response Fund in the LDRRM Investment Plan in accordance with existing guidelines and charge capital outlay for rehabilitation and recovery purposes under the 70% of the fund allocation.

We recommended that the Municipal Supply Officer:
Conduct physical count of all the remaining donated items and issue ICS on semi expendable donations in-kind.
Prepare inventory list of the remaining donated items for DRRM every month or every quarter if the Municipality is under quarantine for more than three months and determine the condition of these items;
Henceforth, prepare all the reports and documents required to account and monitor the receipt and issuances of all donations in kind and submit a copy of all the reports to the Audit Team.

For future transactions involving cash donations, we recommended that:
The Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Officer should prepare the Cash Donation Utilization Request (CDUR) for charges to or utilization of the fund and attach the original copy to the Disbursement Voucher (DV) and furnish the Budget and Accounting Office with one copy each,
The Municipal Accountant should furnish the Municipal Budget Officer copies of the RCD, Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) and credit memo relating to cash donations.
The Municipal Budget Officer use the copies of the RCD, Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) and credit memo as bases in the preparation of the Registry of Cash Donations and Utilization (RCDU).  The RCDU shall be maintained per donor per purpose and updated regularly by the Budget Unit.
The Accounting Unit should prepare and maintain separate subsidiary ledger (SL) for “Trust Liabilities-DRRMF” for cash donations received amounting to ₱100,000.00 and above per donor per purpose while those below ₱100,000.00 shall be grouped and posted in one SL labeled as “Various Donors” per purpose.

For similar transactions in the future we recommend that the Municipal Supply Officer furnish the Municipal Accountant with the duplicate copies of all Acknowledgment Receipts of in-kind donations, invoice receipt and inventory list of donated items for DRRM.
We also recommend that the Municipal Accountant summarize data from the documents submitted by the Municipal Supply Officer according to the required disclosure, compare records with the Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM maintained by the Municipal Supply Officer for check and balance, and disclose the necessary information in the Notes to the Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

Identify the nature and purposes of all land and building owned by the Municipality of Albuera and classify each in the appropriate asset account; and
Record the necessary adjusting entries and disclose the information in the Notes to Financial Statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer: 
obtain every first week of the month the bank statements together with the Debit/Credit Memos, and submit the same to the Municipal Accountant; and
Deposit back the amount of ₱148,981.76 erroneously deducted from SMR and TF.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant: 
Identify and record immediately all transactions in the bank not yet recorded in the books based on the monthly Bank Statements; 
determine differences between book and bank balances and make the necessary adjustments, where appropriate; and 
prepare monthly the Bank Reconciliation Statements for all bank accounts of LGU-Albuera.

We recommended that the official in-charge of AMWSS furnish the Municipal Accountant every 5th day of the month with the billing report of water service fees from all water concessionaires for recording of the total receivables in the books of the LGU.  

Also, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer provide the Municipal Accountant every 5th day of the month with the monthly summary of unpaid rentals from market stalls for recording of the appropriate Receivables in the books of the LGU.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant record within the same month the receivables from water service fees and market stall rentals, provide for the necessary allowance for doubtful accounts, including the related income therefrom.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review in detail the transactions affecting the LDRRMF and record the current year unexpended balance to the Trust Liability-DRRM account.

We further reiterate our recommendation that the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Municipal Accountant, open a separate bank account solely for LDRRMF and deposit therein all the unexpended current and prior years LDRRMF, excluding the Capital Outlay from previous years.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor provide support to the MDRRM Officer and monitor accomplishments of the programs/activities/projects in the LDRRMFIP to improve the readiness of LGU-Albuera in its ability to respond in times of calamities.

We also recommended that the designated MDRRM Officer implement all the activities in the LDRRMIP in order to maximize the utilization of the fund for its intended purpose.

Continue to provide technical assistance to all recipients of the YRRP  and coordinate with DA-RFO 8 representatives for necessary interventions that may be requested by recipient associations to insure continuity and success of the program.

We recommended that the Municipal Agriculturist continue to assist the proponent group so that the program will continue to benefit the intended beneficiaries.  Also, require the proponent group to return the unutilized funds of ₱69,485.94, unless there is a written approval for its utilization.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that:

the Budget Officer prepare a separate and balanced budget for SMR as an economic enterprise and allocate expenditures equal to or less than the estimated revenue;

the Municipal Mayor discuss with the officials concerned regarding the meager collections of SMR, identify all factors that adversely affect its low performance and impose corrective measures and strategies to maximize the use of the resort facilities; and

The Tourism Officer devise tourism information campaign in whatever medium, especially through social media, to promote and increase the public’s awareness about the resort and create other revenue generating activities to increase the resort’s income.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Treasurer delegate and assign as collectors only the permanent RCCs who are properly bonded, who shall not be allowed to further delegate their functions to Casual personnel.

We recommended that the Accountant issue a Certificate of Availability of Funds and attach the same to the contract documents of all procurement undergoing public bidding and as general requirement of all disbursement.

We recommended that the Chairman of the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) require its Secretariat to ensure that all contract agreements and its integral parts are properly accomplished and supported with the necessary information before submitting the same to the Audit Team.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer close the bank accounts maintained for the LP and GOP accounts and return to the General Fund of the LGU the remaining balances. Also, collect the bank charges from the persons responsible for its incurrence.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant: 
 
Process payrolls when the net take home pay of all employees of LGU-Albuera are not below ₱5,000.00; 
 
Refrain from signing loan applications if the mandated net take home pay of the employee is below ₱5,000.00; and

Authorize deductions only if the loan was obtained from accredited financing institutions.

observations.recommendation:
1.14 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor: (1) issue a directive to each
official of LGU-Albuera, who are responsible in the accounting and reporting
of the Local Road Network of the LGU, to accomplish the procedures outlined
in COA Circular No. 2015-008; (2) monitor their compliance thereon; and (3)
submit to COA the correct report at year-end.

1.15 Specifically, we recommended that:
1.15.1 The Municipal Assessor inspect and assess the cost of the road lot
component of all road network under LGU-Albuera jurisdiction using
as guide the report submitted by the Municipal Engineer to the DILG.
1.15.2 The Municipal Engineer:
a) Inspect, appraise and segregate each component of each road
network of the LGU using as guide the report submitted to DILG
and the list from the accounting records;
b) Prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Local Road Network
(RPCLRN) with the complete description and cost of each road
network and its component; and
c) Submit the RPCLRN to COA every year-end, copy furnished the
Municipal Accountant and the General Services Officer.
1.15.3 The Municipal Accountant:
a) Record the Road Lot component of Municipal Road projects
based on the report of the Municipal Assessor;
b) Reconcile the composition of the Road Network account per
books against the RPCLRN and adjust, where necessary;
c) List all barangay road projects implemented by the Municipality
and derecognize their costs in the books of LGU-Albuera;
d) Provide the Punong Barangay and the Municipal Bookkeeper
concerned with the list and cost of said barangay road projects for
recording in their respective books of accounts;
e) Maintain the Local Road Network Ledger Cards with complete
data/description of each component of the; and
f) Disclose in the Notes to FS in the ensuing years the breakdown of
the Road Network account.
1.15.4 The General Services Officer prepare and maintain complete Property
Cards on Road Networks and its components and for every future
procurement of the LGU.

1.16 In their reply dated June 22, 2020, the officials concerned concurred with the given
audit recommendations. Accordingly, the Mayor has already issued a directive and
composed a team headed the Municipal Assessor to assess the value of the road
lots. Likewise, the Office of the Accountant and General Services were also given
directives to follow-up on the output of the Municipal Assessor and record
appropriately as required and recommended above.

2.7. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Identify the nature and purposes of all land and building owned by the
Municipality of Albuera and classify each in the appropriate asset account;
and
b. Record the necessary adjusting entries and disclose the information in the
Notes to Financial Statements.

2.8. The Municipal Accountant concurred with the audit recommendations and
informed the Audit Team that the balance of the Market Account and its
corresponding Accumulated Depreciation was reclassified to Investment Property-
Building and Accumulated Depreciation last May 26, 2020 per JEV No. 1644 as
recommended.

3.12 We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer: (1) obtain every first week of
the month the bank statements together with the Debit/Credit Memos, and
submit the same to the Municipal Accountant; and (2) deposit back the
amount of 148,981.76 erroneously deducted from SMR and TF.

3.13 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant: (1) identify and record
immediately all transactions in the bank not yet recorded in the books based
on the monthly Bank Statements; (2) determine differences between book and
bank balances and make the necessary adjustments, where appropriate; and
(3) prepare monthly the Bank Reconciliation Statements for all bank accounts
of LGU-Albuera.

3.16 The Audit Team further recommended that the Treasurer or the Accountant request
for the snapshot weekly or every 15 days and send a written follow-up with Land
Bank management regarding the status of the LGU's iAccess application to
facilitate monitoring of bank balances.

4.8 We recommended that the official in-charge of AMWSS furnish the Municipal
Accountant every 5th day of the month with the billing report of water service
fees from all water concessionaires for recording of the total receivables in the
books of the LGU.

4.9 Also, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer provide the Municipal
Accountant every 5th day of the month with the monthly summary of unpaid
rentals from market stalls for recording of the appropriate Receivables in the
books of the LGU.

4.10 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant record within the same
month the receivables from water service fees and market stall rentals, provide
for the necessary allowance for doubtful accounts, including the related
income therefrom.

4.11 In their answer dated June 15, 2020, the Municipal Accountant concurred with the
recommendations and will require the offices concerned to submit the receivables
on the recommended schedules so as to record the necessary accruals and its
allowances for doubtful accounts monthly.

5.8. We recommended that the Municipal Accountant review in detail the
transactions affecting the LDRRMF and record the current year unexpended
balance to the Trust Liability-DRRM account.

5.9. We further reiterate our recommendation that the Municipal Treasurer, in
coordination with the Municipal Accountant, open a separate bank account
solely for LDRRMF and deposit therein all the unexpended current and prior
years LDRRMF, excluding the Capital Outlay from previous years.

5.1 In her reply, the Municipal Accountant noted the audit recommendation and agreed
to reconcile her records monthly with the DRRM Office report for accurate recording
of the utilized funds on LDRRMF.

6.10 We recommended that:
6.10.1 The Municipal Development Council evaluate first the proposed
projects recommended by the Barangay Chairpersons before the said
projects are incorporated in the Municipality's Annual Investment
Plan, including the correctness of the project cost;
6.10.2 The Municipal Planning and Development Officer submit a report to
the Municipal Mayor and the Bids and Awards Committee (BAC) of
the list of projects with delayed procurement to resolve the causes of
the delay;
6.10.3 The BAC Chairman speed up the conduct of bidding of the projects
identified in the AIP following the schedule of implementation as
indicated in the Project Procurement Management Plan (PPMP); and
6.10.4 The Municipal Engineer implement and monitor strictly all projects
awarded during the year to ensure their completion within the
timelines specified in the contract/agreement.

6.11 In their reply to the AOM through a letter dated June 15, 2020, management
explained that the programs, projects, and activities for CY 2019 were not duly
implemented because of the following reasons:



The barangays failed to provide their committed counterparts resulting to
insufficient funds for its implementation,

Lot acquisition under Solid Waste Management Program was not realized,
although it met the criteria set by EMB-DENR, because the lot owner failed to
secure land title on time in which is a requirement prior to payment as
prescribed by COA.

Improvement of Water System (drilling of 1 production well) was not realized
because the present administration took into consideration the cost-benefit
issue relative to operation and maintenance cost especially on higher power
consumption and the corresponding prohibitive cost in the replacement of
submersible pump. Furthermore, if said drilling should have been pursued
stability of aquifer or water table is not guaranteed for sufficient supply. Upon
proper consultation and recommendation by the Local Development Council,
47

the present administration caused the re-alignment of the said project output to
augment funds for the alternative source of water suppler (Level III) at
Barangay Sherwood, Albuera Leyte.

6.12 Management, however, gave their assurances that measures were already taken to
facilitate the implementation of the lined-up projects covered under CY 2019
project under the 20% Development Fund.

7.13 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor provide support to the MDRRM
Officer and monitor accomplishments of the programs/activities/projects in
the LDRRMFIP to improve the readiness of LGU-Albuera in its ability to
respond in times of calamities.

7.14 We also recommended that the designated MDRRM Officer implement all
the activities in the LDRRMIP in order to maximize the utilization of the fund
for its intended purpose.

7.15 In her reply, the MDRRM Officer affirmed that the very minimal implementation
may be attributed to the election ban and the subsequent change in administration.
Being aware with the mandate as provided on RA. No. 10121 and DILG Circular,
her office duly noted the recommendations, and assured that their programs,
projects, and activities as prioritized will be duly implemented to serve better their
constituents and become a disaster resilient community.

9.16 We recommended that the Municipal Agriculturist continue to assist the
proponent group so that the program will continue to benefit the intended
beneficiaries. Also, require the proponent group to return the unutilized funds
of 69,485.94, unless there is a written approval for its utilization.

9.17 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant assist the proponent
group in the preparation of the SRE and submit the same to DARFO8 and
other documents to fully liquidate the funds transferred to the LGU.

9.18 The Municipal Agriculturist took note of the audit recommendations and gave his
assurance on continued technical assistance to the PG citing that it is his mandate
to support any PG engaged in Agri-fishery related activities. He informed the Audit
Team that the PG will submit their request to the source agency and secure a written
approval therefrom to utilize the unused funds instead of returning it back to the
source agency.

10.17 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
10.17.1 enforce strictly the provisions of Resolution 2017-123 and the Revenue
Code of Albuera through the issuance of Demand Letters to all
delinquent water consumers and market stallholders; and
10.17.2 Discuss and resolve, with the official in-charge of AMWSS, the
difficulties encountered in the operations of the water system and
improve the technical services provided to problem areas so all users
receive consistent and satisfactory water supply.

10.18 We also recommended that the official in-charge of AMWSS:
10.18.1. require the AMWSS programmer to conduct a workshop on the
functions of the program used to record and monitor the waterworks
transactions to familiarize the employee-users;
10.18.2. generate monthly reports that summarizes unpaid accounts and the
delinquent payees and submit to the Municipal Accountant;
10.18.3. prepare Demand Letters monthly to all delinquent water consumers
for signature of the Mayor; and
10.18.4. Issue Notices of Disconnection to water consumers who fail to adhere
with the Demand Letters.

10.19 Further, we recommended that the Municipal Treasurer:
10.19.1. maintain a registry book to monitor stall applicants;
10.19.2. collect on the specified dates all rentals due from all market
stallholders, with surcharges, if necessary;
10.19.3. send monthly written notices to delinquent stallholders; and
10.19.4. Cancel the contract of lease of space or stall whose rental fee has not
been paid for three (3) consecutive months and declare such space or
stall vacant.

10.20 In their answer dated June 16, 2020, the Municipal Treasurer concurred and noted
our recommendations. She said that they will give appropriate attention on the time
and manner of collection for rental fees in accordance with the Revenue Code, and
promised that they will now prepare the summary of monthly unpaid rental for
appropriate recording of the Accounting Office.

10.21 The AMWSS supervisor, in his letter reply dated June 19, 2020, also said that a
meeting was already conducted to systematically resolve the inadequacies noted.
Accordingly, he will designate personnel to focus on monitoring and firmly
implement the established policies on collections and disconnection. Also with the
latest acquisition of new billing system, it will help the office to be more accurate
and efficient in delivering bills that will help increase collection as well as in the
preparation of report as recommended. With regards to the issuance of the
Disconnection Notice, the supervisor said that he already designated a personnel
who will focus on its preparation and in implementing the disconnection of water
service to customers who failed to pay their bills.

11.10 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor convene the Market Committee
and require them to:
11.10.1. Compute the appropriate rates for each market section that can
cover all the operating costs and yield a reasonable revenues;
11.10.2. Update the Revenue Code of the Municipality specifically the rates,
fees and charges to be imposed;
11.10.3. Submit the proposed revisions to the Sangguniang Bayan, with the
Municipal Mayor recommending its approval.

11.11 We also recommended that the Municipal Vice Mayor, as the Presiding Officer
of the Sangguniang Bayan, facilitate the immediate issuance of a local revenue
ordinance for the adoption of the revised rates and charges of the Municipality.

11.12 Further, we recommended that the Budget Officer properly allocate cost of
improvement, repair and other related expenses attributable to Market
operations.

12.11 We recommended that:
12.11.1. the Budget Officer prepare a separate and balanced budget for
SMR as an economic enterprise and allocate expenditures equal to
or less than the estimated revenue;
12.11.2. the Municipal Mayor discuss with the officials concerned regarding
the meager collections of SMR, identify all factors that adversely
affect its low performance and impose corrective measures and
strategies to maximize the use of the resort facilities; and
12.11.3. The Tourism Officer devise tourism information campaign in
whatever medium, especially through social media, to promote and
increase the public's awareness about the resort and create other
revenue generating activities to increase the resort's income.

12.12 The Municipal Treasurer, in her answer dated June 16, 2020, noted our findings
and concurred with the recommendations. She said that the decrease may have
been due to its temporary closure on November 2018 which might have caused
some confusion to the public on the continuity of SMR operation. She assured the
Audit Team that they will make thorough analysis and consider all circumstances
that may affect in setting up their target.

13.9. We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Municipal Treasurer
delegate and assign as collectors only the permanent RCCs who are properly
bonded, who shall not be allowed to further delegate their functions to Casual
personnel.

14.6. We recommended that the Accountant issue a Certificate of Availability of
Funds and attach the same to the contract documents of all procurement
undergoing public bidding and as general requirement of all disbursement.

15.5. We recommended that the Chairman of the Bids and Awards Committee

integral parts are properly accomplished and supported with the necessary
information before submitting the same to the Audit Team.

maintained for the LP and GOP accounts and return to the General Fund of

the LGU the remaining balances. Also, collect the bank charges from the

SB Resolution authorizing her to close the accounts as required by the bank and

of bank charges, she informed the Audit Team that the Municipality is considering

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor adopt the following remedies to address the aforesaid problems:

Require the Municipal Accountant to record the accurate amount of RPT and SET Receivables at the start of the yearusing as basis the Municipal Assessor’s report on taxable assessed value of real properties in accordance with Chapters IV and V of RA No. 7160.

Require the Municipal Assessor:
conduct continuous ocular inspections at the barangay level to validate the actual use of the real properties;
update the Assessment Rolls as to the conditions of the real properties, the exact addresses and identity of declared owners, the exemptions on RPT, if any;
update continuously its records particularly on the Tax Declarations issued and check if there are clerical errors due to General Revisions.

Direct the Municipal Treasurer to:
develop an effective Tax Collection Campaign program to intensify and maximize collection of Real Property/Special Education Tax Receivables.
deputize the Barangay Treasurers, in addition to the collectors of the Municipality, to collect all taxes on real property located in the Barangay, if properly bonded for the purpose.
Implement the procedures prescribed in Section 269 of the RA 7160 on delinquent accounts.

We also recommended that Municipal Mayor mandate every employee of the Municipal Government to assist the Municipal Treasurer’s effort in its campaign to increase collections of real property taxes in order to increase the Municipality’s income and improve its financial capacity.
